structured_choices.data_source: Electronic health records (EHR) from a single hospital system covering 100,000 patient cases over three years. Clinical notes, discharge summaries, and follow-up reports are extracted. Data is stratified by discharge date to ensure temporal representation.
structured_choices.label_definition: Multi-class outcome variable: post-discharge complications identified within 30 days of discharge, extracted from clinical documentation, structured follow-up visits, and readmission records. Classes are defined a priori (e.g., infection, cardiac, respiratory, bleeding, other/none). Any patient with multiple complications is assigned to the primary (most severe or first-occurring) complication class. Label source must be independent of the input clinical notes used for prediction — specifically, labels are drawn from structured follow-up data and readmission flags, not inferred retroactively from discharge summary text.
structured_choices.split_method: Temporal stratified split. The dataset is ordered by discharge date. The first 60% (approximately 60,000 patients, spanning ~22 months) is used for training. The next 20% (approximately 20,000 patients, spanning ~7 months) is used for validation. The final 20% (approximately 20,000 patients, spanning ~7 months) is held out as test. Within each split, stratification by complication class is applied to preserve class distribution. Temporal split prevents information leakage from future clinical updates or follow-up documentation influencing training. Stratification within each temporal window prevents class imbalance from skewing the validation and test sets, enabling fair evaluation across all complication types.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing transformers (tokenizer, text normalizer, vocabulary builder for the transformer model) are fit exclusively on the training set. Validation and test sets are processed using the training-fit transformers without refitting. Scaling or normalization of any structured features (e.g., age, lab values extracted from notes) is fit on training data only and applied to validation and test. This prevents data leakage and ensures the model encounters realistic out-of-distribution text in validation and test.
structured_choices.features: Primary features are raw clinical notes and discharge summaries, tokenized and embedded via a transformer encoder (e.g., pre-trained BioBERT or ClinicalBERT). Secondary structured features (if available and useful) include patient demographics (age, sex), admission type, length of stay, and count of comorbidities extracted from problem lists or structured fields—not inferred from note text. No engineered features derived from outcome labels or future follow-up notes are included. Text preprocessing includes lowercasing, removal of Protected Health Information (PHI) patterns, and handling of special characters; this is applied consistently across all splits after fitting the tokenizer on training data.
structured_choices.model: A transformer-based sequence classification model: either a fine-tuned pre-trained clinical BERT variant (e.g., ClinicalBERT, BioBERT) with a classification head for multi-class output, or an equivalent transformer encoder with attention pooling and a softmax classification layer. The model is trained end-to-end with appropriate regularization (dropout, weight decay) to mitigate overfitting on the training set. Hyperparameters (learning rate, batch size, number of epochs, dropout rate, warmup steps) are tuned using the validation set.
structured_choices.baseline: Logistic regression trained on TF-IDF features extracted from the same clinical notes (using the same training set to fit the TF-IDF vectorizer). The logistic regression receives the full vocabulary of unigrams and bigrams from training notes, scaled to the same dimensionality as reasonably feasible. Logistic regression hyperparameters (L2 regularization strength, class weights to handle imbalance) are tuned via grid search on the validation set using the same primary metric as the transformer model. This ensures the baseline receives equivalent feature engineering effort and hyperparameter tuning. Both models are evaluated under identical conditions: same train/val/test split, same metric, same class weighting strategy for handling imbalance.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Macro-averaged F1 score. Justification: In a multi-class imbalanced setting, macro-F1 ensures that performance across all complication classes is weighted equally, preventing the model from optimizing primarily for the majority class. This aligns with the clinical objective: clinicians must reliably identify rare but serious complications (e.g., cardiac events) as well as common ones. Macro-F1 rewards balanced precision and recall across all classes, making it appropriate for targeted interventions. Accuracy alone would be misleading if one class dominates.
secondary_metrics: Per-class F1 scores: to detect whether the transformer gains primarily on common classes or achieves improvements across all classes
Macro-averaged precision and recall: to distinguish whether improvements come from fewer false positives (precision) or better identification of true cases (recall)
Weighted F1 (weighted by class frequency): to contextualize improvements relative to base-rate performance

Calibration: confidence scores (from softmax) are compared to true class proportions in validation and test; ECE (Expected Calibration Error) is reported to assess whether the transformer is overconfident
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Hyperparameter tuning is performed using the validation set (20% of data). The primary metric (macro-F1) is computed on the validation set for every candidate hyperparameter configuration. The configuration with the highest validation macro-F1 is selected. Early stopping may be employed during training using validation loss or validation macro-F1 to prevent overfitting. The test set is used only once, at the end, after all model selection is finalized. No information from the test set is used to guide hyperparameter choice.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set is held completely separate and touched only once. After model selection is complete on the validation set, the final tuned transformer and logistic regression models are evaluated on the test set to obtain unbiased estimates of generalization performance. Test set results are reported with 95% confidence intervals (computed via stratified bootstrap) and are the primary basis for accepting or rejecting the hypothesis.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Physician documentation quality variation is addressed through multiple mechanisms: (1) Temporal stratification ensures training, validation, and test sets span the same time period, reducing the risk that systematic improvements in documentation over time confound the comparison. (2) Both the transformer and baseline receive identical raw notes, so systematic documentation biases affect both models equally. The comparison isolates the model architecture's contribution. (3) A sensitivity analysis is planned: models are retrained on a stratified subsample of notes pre-reviewed for documentation quality (manual quality flags), and results are compared to the full-data results. If transformer gains persist in the high-quality subset, documentation noise is less likely to be the driver. (4) If possible, cross-site validation is performed: if additional data from another hospital system with different documentation patterns is available, the trained models are evaluated on external data to assess robustness. (5) Class imbalance is controlled through stratified splits and balanced class weighting during training (e.g., via class_weight='balanced' in logistic regression and weighted cross-entropy loss in the transformer). This ensures rare complications are not systematically underrepresented.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: Transformer vs. Logistic Regression for Post-Discharge Complication Prediction

### 1. Objective and Scope
This experiment tests whether a transformer-based model trained on raw clinical notes predicts post-discharge complications (within 30 days) with higher multi-class classification performance than a traditional logistic regression baseline. Success is measured by comparison on held-out test data, controlling for model selection bias and ensuring fair baseline tuning. The experiment does not establish causality (notes do not cause complications) but rather evaluates predictive accuracy under realistic deployment conditions.

### 2. Data Strategy
The analysis uses 100,000 EHR patient cases spanning three years. Clinical notes and discharge summaries are the primary input; follow-up reports and readmission flags provide the outcome labels (multi-class complication type or none within 30 days). 

**Critical:** Labels are defined a priori and extracted from structured follow-up documentation and readmission records, not inferred from discharge summary text. This prevents the outcome from being encoded in the input features. Each patient receives a single complication class label (the primary or most severe complication) to create a well-defined multi-class target.

### 3. Temporal Stratified Split
Data is ordered chronologically by discharge date and split as follows:
- **Training set (60%):** 60,000 patients (~22 months of early data)
- **Validation set (20%):** 20,000 patients (~7 months of middle data)
- **Test set (20%):** 20,000 patients (~7 months of recent data)

Within each split, stratification by complication class preserves the class distribution, preventing imbalance from artificially inflating or deflating performance estimates. Temporal ordering prevents future clinical information (later follow-ups, evolving treatment outcomes) from leaking into training. This design reflects the real deployment scenario: the model will predict on newly discharged patients using only data available at discharge.

### 4. Preprocessing and Feature Engineering
All text preprocessing transformers (tokenizer, normalizer, vocabulary builder) are fit on the training set only. Validation and test data are processed using these training-fit transformers without refitting. This ensures the model encounters realistic out-of-distribution text and avoids leakage.

Raw clinical notes are used directly as inputs, with standard hygiene: lowercasing, removal of Protected Health Information (PHI) patterns, and handling of special characters. If structured features (age, admission type, length of stay, comorbidity count) are available, these are included for both models; any numerical scaling is fit on training data and applied to validation and test.

### 5. Model Architecture and Baseline
**Model:** A fine-tuned pre-trained clinical transformer (e.g., ClinicalBERT or BioBERT) with a classification head for multi-class output. The transformer is trained end-to-end on the training set with appropriate regularization (dropout, weight decay). Class weighting is applied in the loss function to handle complication class imbalance.

**Baseline:** Logistic regression trained on TF-IDF features extracted from the same raw clinical notes. The TF-IDF vectorizer is fit on training data, then applied to validation and test. Logistic regression hyperparameters (L2 regularization, class weights) are tuned via grid search on the validation set using the same evaluation metric as the transformer. This ensures the baseline receives equivalent effort: same feature source (raw notes), equivalent preprocessing, and equivalent hyperparameter optimization.

### 6. Hyperparameter Tuning and Model Selection
Both models are tuned using the validation set. The primary evaluation metric (macro-averaged F1 score) is computed on the validation set for every candidate hyperparameter configuration. The configuration with the highest validation macro-F1 is selected. The test set remains untouched during tuning and is used only once, at the end, to report final performance and confidence intervals.

### 7. Evaluation Metrics
**Primary metric:** Macro-averaged F1 score. Justification: In a multi-class imbalanced setting (some complications are rare), macro-F1 treats all classes equally, preventing the model from optimizing solely on common complications. This aligns with clinical priorities: identifying rare but serious complications is as important as identifying common ones. A transformer that improves on rare classes while degrading on common classes would show positive macro-F1 gain.

**Secondary metrics:**
- Per-class F1 scores: reveal whether gains are uniform or concentrated
- Weighted F1: contextualizes improvements relative to class prevalence
- Calibration error (ECE): assesses whether model confidence scores align with true class frequencies, important for clinical decision-support

### 8. Confound Controls
Physician documentation quality variation could bias results. The design addresses this through:
- **Temporal stratification:** Training, validation, and test sets span the same time period, reducing the confound of systematic documentation improvements over time.
- **Symmetric treatment:** Both models receive identical raw notes, so systematic documentation biases affect both equally. Any difference isolates the model architecture's contribution.
- **Sensitivity analysis:** If documentation quality labels are available, models are retrained on a high-quality subset and results are compared to full-data results. Persistent transformer gains suggest robustness to documentation noise.
- **Class balance:** Stratified splits and weighted loss functions ensure rare complications are not systematically underrepresented during training.

### 9. Expected Outcomes and Interpretation
The test set macro-F1 scores for the transformer and baseline are compared. If the transformer's test macro-F1 exceeds the baseline by a clinically meaningful margin (e.g., ≥2 percentage points), the hypothesis is supported. Per-class F1 and calibration metrics clarify whether gains are broad-based and reliable. If gains are marginal, concentrated on rare classes only, or accompanied by poor calibration, the practical value is questioned.
